Neptune Instance Resource Allocation Overview

Amazon EC2 instance types and sizes used in Neptune offer a defined amount of compute (vCPUs) and system memory.
Amazon EC2 is a compute service.
Neptune is a database service.
The primary storage for Neptune is external to the DB instances in a cluster.
Neptune's external primary storage allows compute and storage capacity to scale independently of each other.
In all Neptune instance families, vCPU resources are allocated to support two (2) query execution threads per vCPU.
The number of query execution threads supported per vCPU in Neptune is dictated by the instance size.
When determining the proper size of a Neptune DB instance, the possible concurrency of the application must be considered.
When determining the proper size of a Neptune DB instance, the average latency of queries must be considered.
The number of vCPUs needed for a Neptune DB instance can be estimated using the formula: vCPUs = (latency x concurrency) / 2.
In the Neptune vCPU formula, latency is measured as the average query latency in seconds.
In the Neptune vCPU formula, concurrency is measured as the target number of queries per second.
SPARQL queries can, under certain circumstances, use more than one execution thread per query.
openCypher queries can, under certain circumstances, use more than one execution thread per query.
Gremlin read queries that use the DFE (Data Flow Engine) query engine can, under certain circumstances, use more than one execution thread per query.
When initially sizing a Neptune DB cluster, the assumption should be that each query will consume a single execution thread per execution.
A Neptune DB cluster should be scaled up if back pressure into the query queue is observed.
Back pressure into the Neptune query queue can be observed using the `/gremlin/status` API.
Back pressure into the Neptune query queue can be observed using the `/oc/status` API.
Back pressure into the Neptune query queue can be observed using the `/sparql/status` API.
Back pressure into the Neptune query queue can be observed using the `MainRequestsPendingRequestsQueue` CloudWatch metric.
CloudWatch is a monitoring service.
System memory on each Neptune instance is divided into two primary allocations: buffer pool cache and query execution thread memory.
Approximately two thirds of the available memory in a Neptune instance is allocated for buffer pool cache.
Buffer pool cache is used to cache the most recently used components of the graph for faster access on queries that repeatedly access those components.
Neptune instances with a larger amount of system memory have larger buffer pool caches that can store more of the graph locally.
A user can tune for the appropriate amount of buffer pool cache by monitoring the buffer cache hit and miss metrics available in CloudWatch.
The size of a Neptune instance should be increased if the cache hit rate drops below 99.9% for a consistent period of time.
A cache hit rate below 99.9% for a consistent period of time suggests that the buffer pool is not big enough.
A cache hit rate below 99.9% for a consistent period of time suggests the Neptune engine is having to fetch data from the underlying storage volume more often than is efficient.
The remaining third of system memory in a Neptune instance is distributed evenly across query execution threads.
Some memory in a Neptune instance remains for the operating system.
Some memory in a Neptune instance is reserved as a small dynamic pool for threads to use as needed.
The memory available for each Neptune query execution thread increases slightly from one instance size to the next up to an `8xl` instance type.
At the `8xl` instance type, the memory allocated per thread in Neptune reaches a maximum.
The time to add more thread memory in Neptune is when an `OutOfMemoryException` (OOM) is encountered.
An `OutOfMemoryException` (OOM) in Neptune occurs when one thread needs more than the maximum memory allocated to it.
An `OutOfMemoryException` (OOM) in Neptune is not the same as the entire instance running out of memory.
